transcript.text: I'm soon, but I did some digging on the notes from the call you had back in March.
I just didn't have like a recording available because we've like switched to a new CRM.
From what I understand, you guys acquired some companies, you're merging together like 14,000 pages, and a lot of the opportunity that you saw with GrowthX was around content refreshes.
Is that right?
Yeah, I think in general, so the company, so James leads Growth Marketing, and I joined in January.
James was sort of consulting with the company for a couple months before that.
Chanel joined, I don't know when, Chanel, May or June?
June, yeah, about three months ago.

And so kind of the situation we inherited is the company grew quickly for, it was probably, I don't know, it's a dozen years old, maybe 15 years old, grew pretty fast for a while, and then over the last four or five years did.
Yeah.
A big bunch of acquisitions, grew the portfolio significantly.
But one of the things that we inherited was, for whatever reason, there were some suboptimal decisions in terms of like the way we architected the website.
So the blog was not on the same domain and things like that.
So long story short, you got it.
Basically, we're converging a number of acquired websites.
We're replatforming the web onto Sanity, CMS.
We're bringing the blog over to the main domain.
We're bringing the community to the main domain.
And so I think the piece that we were thinking about is, and the other thing is, we're kind of playing in multiple product categories.
So you'll see on our website, there's six solution areas.
They're pretty much representative of the different categories we play in, and whoever was kind of doing SEO.
Well, there wasn't GEO a year or ago, but whoever was doing it, we weren't focused on those categories at the time.
So there wasn't really content out there to grow, let's say, our positioning around DSPM, which is a space that, like Sierra, is a hot performer in.
We're not even ranking anywhere near that, even though we probably have equal or higher revenues there.
Same with some of the other security categories that we have.
So we reoriented around these six solution areas in end of April, May timeframe, roughly.
We've built out those webpages.
They're starting to get traffic now, which is good, and the traffic is growing.
But we have a long way to go, and we're still, like, on page six for DSPM and stuff like that.
That's just for SEO.
And obviously, AEO, GEO, whatever you want to call it.
We're also making some strides there, but there's a lot more to go.
And we have a very small content marketing team.
And so they're only able to do so much.
And so we're trying to figure out, I attended the two-hour workshop, whenever that was, a while back.
And we're trying to figure out, I like the approach of using AI to scale, and obviously tailoring very carefully for your specific personas that we're dealing with.
And because we play in different categories, we actually have some pretty distinct personas that we're going after.
And so maybe, you know, sort of learning from you guys in terms of what's worked with some of the other players that you have, and getting us jump-started, getting us going, was my thinking.
And we didn't really feel like it made sense to do that until the new website was on the new platform, and it'll be much more optimized for tech performance as well.
As having all the pieces in a folder structure and everything that's going to be more optimized.
So that goes live October 15th.
We're sort of going through staging server pages now.
And so I wanted to kind of reconnect and say, okay, what would be a good time to get started with you guys?
Probably good since Chanel wasn't on the original call.
And even I know things change on your site as well to just do a quick refresh on what you guys are doing these days, what you're seeing.

What I was thinking, what struck me heavily on the workshop call was, you know, I forget if it was Marcel or James or whoever provided the example of sort of creating a guide for a long tail sort of question or how to solve a problem.
I don't remember what the example was, but using that as a kind of way to scale up and create a guide that was showing up, I think, even the next day as an answer in one of the GPT engines.
And so thinking that through, like, you know, we don't have the capacity to do it all ourselves.
So it's kind of thinking about how can we help scale?

And obviously, we want to make progress on the category level searches, but some of the ways that that might also, you know, we might get further faster is taking some of those specific high intent use cases and picking our way through some of those and scaling out guides and things like that for some of those.
So anyway, that was what caught my eye.
And obviously, I worked closely with Marcel in past lives and trust him.
And I love where you guys are going.
So just maybe you could do a quick.
Level set for James and Chanel and where you guys are today, and then we can figure out what might make sense for next steps.
Yeah, yeah, absolutely, John.
Thanks for all the context.
That's super helpful, and it's crazy how much progress y'all have made since March.
I'm sure you're glad to be close to the finish line on a lot of this.

It's going to be, you know, we're looking at, you know, the NetRix site is, looks, takes us back to the 90s sort of view, and so it's going to be much more modern, much more interesting in a couple weeks, thanks to a lot of effort from these two others on the call, so.
Well, the future is getting here faster than we know it, so that sounds good.
Chanel, great to meet you, just to kind of get to know, you know, you and your role a little bit better.
Would you mind doing a quick intro?
I'm on the sales side here at GrowthX.
You probably know what that entails.
Yeah, yeah.
So, my name is Chanel, and I lead...
Product Marketing.
So the reason I'm on this call is really to understand how we can adjust our messaging to really help turbocharge the growth that you're trying to do.
So I'm really excited to hear what you have to share.
Perfect.
And do you have any context on what we do at GrowthX or is this totally fresh for you?
So John mentioned a little bit yesterday, which is why he added me to this call.
I was not originally on it.
But yeah, I have a little bit of context.
But, you know, whatever else you can share that might assist me in helping you would be great.
Yeah.
And part of the other context, Tyler, is content marketing reports into James.
But I thought the reason I brought Chanel on is there's got to be this sort of coordination between content marketing, product marketing on some of the use cases that we want to go after.
And when we're thinking about category level, like if we're talking about identity security, for example, like.
There's interesting use cases, say, around Active Directory recovery and an attack or whatever that ChanelChambers is pretty close to.
so just that, as we start to think about what are the best levers we have for growth, I want to make sure Chanel's aware of what we're cooking up here.
Perfect.
That makes sense.
Oh, yeah.
Go for it, James.
Tyler, I just going to add one more piece to the puzzle that we're solving.
mean, so the team that was here prior working on SEO, I think they kind of fell into a common trap.
They were kind of chasing a vanity metric.
lot of, you know, they're really looking at traffic and not kind of chasing it down funnel, which in my mind can be a very expensive mistake.
You know, so one of the things that we've been really focused on recently that's allowed us to kind of turn the tide, when John and I came into the organization, we had been on a very long downward.
We had been a very downward.
Slide in terms of both organic traffic and also organic originated pipeline.
You know, my big push is like, look, like we need to make sure that we're focused on, you know, areas that matter most.
so, you know, we, you know, rather than moving 100 things an inch, let's move, you 10 things a mile.
And so as we were engaging the vendor that's building the new website for us, they have a SEO team.

You know, my big push is let's focus on, you know, high converting areas of the site, you know, product pages, things that are much more, you know, conversion oriented than kind of top of funnel, you know, research discovery.

Not to say that we shouldn't be looking at the entire picture, but it's sort of like if we have to pick our battles, let's make sure we are doing that and not getting distracted by, you know, lots of shiny objects.
So NetNet, kind of where we're at right now, we did turn the tide on pipeline first.
We continue to see down.
We were slide on traffic, mostly because of the blog.
We saw, you know, actual up and to the right on a lot of our kind of key conversion-oriented pages.
We saw homepage traffic going up and to the right, but the blog continued to slide.
And a lot of that, you know, we surmise as, you know, a lot of other companies are feeling as well as, you know, AI snippets are, you know, stealing, you know, attention and eyeballs, right?
But we are starting to turn the tide on traffic as well, so things are finally starting to go up and to the right on that metric as well.

But I think the big thing for me that it's been a while since we last spoke, you know, I'm very mindful of, like, the vanity metric and, like, the hard metric that is going to get the, you know, attention of the sales org, the attention of the board, right?

And so I think as we're having the conversation today, I'd also just love to get a, you know, get a sense of, like, how you think about, like, bifurcating time and attention resources on stuff that tends to be more.

We're more top of funnel and it's more of like that denominator metric versus the stuff that tends to be much more mid to bottom of funnel oriented that allows us to very quickly say, hey, we're making this investment in this vendor.
Here's what we're able to get out of it definitively.
Is that fair?
Yeah, totally fair.
Makes a lot of sense.
I think one thing to say up front is that we always focus on editorial as the foundation and the first part of the partnership.
And so a lot of what we might create for you guys would be on the blog or would be refreshing existing content.
But by no means is that our team is only going to focus on driving traffic, right?
We're looking at driving higher intent traffic and then what's the progression from there?
And are you showing up in LLMs and can we write like a tribute to conversions to the content that we create?
And so, you know, maybe there is a use case where we're doing something programmatic with you.
Maybe there.
Is a use case where we're creating content that's connected to more of the product pages or solutions pages.
But in terms of where we would focus first, it would be more editorial, blog, guides, that kind of content type.
To me, guides and blogs potentially are different.

Because if I'm, I could say a high intent, like someone's trying to solve a problem, and you pop a guide up, and that guide, you know, is closely connected to your solution, that feels a little lower funnel than, say, a blog that might be more like, hey, we're going to get you there for the first time to find out about us.
But I might be wrong.
I don't know what you guys are seeing.
A hundred percent, and I think that both of those scenarios would be content that we would create that would be editorial in nature.
So I don't, so I'm, so I'm not saying that we wouldn't tackle that kind of content.
It can be, right, both sides, thought leadership.

But also like articles, right, because you want to do daily publishing to be able to have a surface area that's wide enough to think about all the ways that, you know, your ICP might interact with ChatGPT and all the different variations, right, in which someone could go in and query.
So it's kind of a combination, I would say.
Well, let me go through a quick presentation, Chanel, just to get you more up to speed, and John and James to catch you up on how our partnership and our platform as well has evolved.
Cool.
Perfect.
So as you've seen, John, right, in some of these workshops and from some of the commentary up front, in this new world, every team is getting less clicks to the website, right?
And the first sales conversation is happening with ChatGPT or something similar.
And it's almost like, you know, your ICP is sort of seeing this as if you're not...
The answer, you don't exist.
And so that's why part of our focus is if we can help you get in the answer quickly, then we can drive higher intent organic traffic to your site.
We've seen that AI search visits are 4.4 times more likely to convert.
They spend three times as long.
And so our approach, since it's audience first, there's benefits on the SEO and the GEO side.
And the reason that we've had a lot of success showing up in AI Answers quickly is because, one, we're able to do quality with scale.
And two, every piece of content that we create is going through the most important factors.
So when you think about doing deep research, but not just leveraging like one researcher, right?

We leverage perplexity, deep research, EXA, Tavli, lots of different, you know, models kind of coming together in one workflow where we're flexible to choose ones that are performing better and always.
Keeping a close eye on how we can refine the workflows that we use.
Can you attribute the content to credible sources?
Is it comprehensive?
Is it fresh?
We recommend refreshing your content every three months as a good barometer.
And so, you know, what you've got with GrowthX, as you've learned from Marcel, is an end-to-end partner who can do everything from SEO and GEO strategy to staging and publishing and your CMS.
And so, all of the last mile execution, in addition to the LLM visibility and analytics.
And so, you know, what we can do for you guys is basically be an extension of your content team, being able to publish at the same quality, but also being able to do that on a daily basis.
Any quick questions at a high level, Chanel?
know this is a recap, John and James.
Yeah, I don't want to get too far into it.
But, I mean, when you say, so on the previous slide, it said, you know, well-researched and attributed and all of that kind of
So what kind of specific subject matter expertise do you look for in order to do that?
Like, would that be where we would come in?
Or do you have your own writers?
Or tell me a little bit more about how we get the quality of the raw material to be higher.
That's great question.
So a lot of it comes down to the context engineering.
And so what we do in our engagements, we do in phase one, an eight-week strategy sprint, where we are meeting with some experts from your team.
We're ingesting different resources.
So we can ingest sales decks, transcripts, brand documents.
We can store this in, like, our knowledge base in the platform.
And so there's these artifacts around your personas, your company, your brand voice.
And then there's a knowledge base of the resources that can help us be more of an expert on your business.
And then there's the expert interviews that we would set up in phase one.
And so that's how we adjust for some of the technical expertise that's needed.
And what's really interesting about our approach is that we work with a ton of technical clients.
I think technical clients has been our sweet spot because of how sophisticated we can make some of the content.
But it is a partnership.
Absolutely.
Yeah.
And so you said you work with, you interview the SMEs.
Do you use a standard interview guide or would we have to put that together for our own engagement?
Oh, yeah.
You wouldn't have to do anything like that.
Okay.
We do use a standard.
Okay, cool.
Process and questions that we would ask with every single client.
Exactly.
But our process, what's cool about it is that we've got our internal platform where our team is actually executing on the content creation, where the AI workflows that we've built live.
And in the next month or two, we're actually bringing clients into the platform so that a lot of the collaboration that we would do.
And we're it.
Tools like Slack, Notion, Airtable can start to happen in a central source of truth.
And so I'll show you the platform and how things have changed.
But the reason that GrowthX is really different than the standard agency partner is this combination of the workflows and then the experts in the loop.
And so, John, I think Marcel mentioned in like a follow-up that we had hired our chief content officer, who was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch for several years.
Every client that we work with gets a managing director, a managing editor, and a content manager.
And they're folks who have either built websites like Jacob, who was an early G2 employee, or folks who have like run SEO for places like Fanatics and TripAdvisor.
And so it's this combination with Marcel's history of always building content engines as opposed to spending on ads.
That's the secret sauce that has like...
Like led to GrowthX having a ton of traction in the market.
I'll go into some content examples and show you the platform and how it's evolved.
But any other questions about what we're doing here or what we're, you know, how we're trying to be different at GrowthX?
Yeah, I do.
I do.
You know, it's great to see the, you know, the hockey stick curves.
to James' point, we're definitely in it for conversion first and kind of overall traffic second.
That being said, we also have an awareness challenge.
So I look at part of this as a little bit of both.
But if we only hit the awareness side on organic traffic without hitting conversion, we wouldn't be happy.
I mean, I'll say the team, the prior team did some vanity metric stuff like ranking for things that are really not even core to what we do.
And we're getting great traffic from it.
But it's like, oh, PowerShell script searches and like.
Okay, but that's not what we do as a company.
So why are we, you know, so great, we had that, we have a lot of visits for that particular thing, but like very hard to monetize that in any way, right?
So I would want to make sure, and as we look at examples, like how are you taking this traffic?
And then how, are there any of your customers who have, you know, are also seeing conversions follow a curve like this as well?
Makes a ton of sense, John.
And when we are putting together these artifacts, right, we're really trying to create them in a way that is thinking about everything that your ICP might care about, right?
So how to win their trust, how to lose their trust, what is going to help get them promoted, what their big pain points are.
And so it's a combination of this deep persona analysis and then topic discovery that ensures.
There's that we're only focusing on creating content in the areas that are relevant, right?
To your ICP and to what is going to get folks to make that next logical conversion step.
In terms of some conversion numbers, I know Marcel has more of this handy.
I've got more of the case studies around traffic, just to tell you the truth.
But one thing that I've heard him mention is that with augment code, so we built this guide section that has now three apps to the traffic of their blog.
They wanted to separate us from the work that they were doing internally.
And these guides, we've published about 100 over a three-month period.
They wanted us to move super fast.
And the guides are probably what Marcel was showing in terms of content that's showing up in AI search, right?
Enterprise AI coding tools, augment wasn't showing up, but companies like Cursor were.
And that was like a big win for them in the early days with us.
Yeah.
They had over 300 signups last week in terms of like an all-time high from what they'd seen converting from some of their editorial pieces.
And so that was a success story we had with Augment.
And everything that you're seeing here, including the images, we're creating for them.
We'll also do like cross-linking with all the content that we create.
And so we can get very technical, like the Augment examples, but we can also think about like integrations pages.
We've built out several of Webflow's integrations pages here.
We're ingesting like their docs to be able to properly accurate, to be able to properly represent what you can do with Webflow and the integration partner.
We've also done things like for Ramp, we created all of their vendor pages.
And so in these vendor pages...
You know, there's tool reviews, but there's also comparisons, alternatives.

And so this is an interesting play that's probably more focused on, right, like the buyer who's thinking about, okay, what tool should we use?
We'll ramp.
They've got all this data on how much every tool costs.
Why, you know, why wouldn't we go with them?
That's cool.
Yeah, mean, certainly if we can come up with, and I don't know what the answer is, but comparison pages or things like that that rank.
I mean, certainly we'd be interested in, and we have some competitive comparison pages, because we're not afraid to do that in our space.
We're actually happy to do that.
We're not like a big brand that doesn't want to give airtime to our competitors.
So I do think those kind of things, and then maybe there's some related ones, like, you know, we have integrations, we have environments we scan, maybe there's...
Some best things we can provide for the technical users that give them insights into these different environments.
I'm not sure you have to think about it, but that's partly, Chanel, why I've got you on here as well.

Because I think thinking beyond blogs, let's say, to these other areas of the site where someone who's looking for that specificity might be more likely to be someone who would convert than, hey, I'm getting some general info off of a blog.
Well, even when I look at the raw material that you are going to require, so the persona data, the information about the value props, differentiators, all of that is going to come from my team.
And I guess my question to you, Tyler, is when you work with other clients, who's providing that stuff?
Is it usually a product marketing team, or do you end up having to create it yourself?
I'm really curious.
Yeah, so the artifacts that we would put together would be something that our team would create.
Using the resources that you share, there's no requirement, right?
On like who shares the resources or what resources we get.
It just speeds up the process, right?

And so when you look at augment code, you know, we created an artifact on their products and services that really just got to the core of the features, the differentiation, and had like the information that ultimately mattered the most.
But in some scenarios, like we'll go really deep, right?
Like maybe we'll do something like this proofreader checklist where we're talking about the specific sources that should be leveraged, the way that the content's written.
And so we can use a lot of different customizations from one client to the next.
And it doesn't have to be as formatted as you might be thinking, right?
It's really more of a matter of can we get a good enough amount of resources to understand your company, your product, and your ICP.
Yeah, one of the things that's going through my mind, Chanel, is even how deep some of our own people really are on some of these personas.
I think the research that we just did, James and Chanel, it was just that focus group on a few of those web pages for that CRO project.
There's some little good nuggets inside of that, right?
What's in the mind?
How are they perceiving information?

And Chanel, what I'm showing you here is a look at the knowledge base and how we set up a knowledge graph for retrieval to just make things more semantically relevant when you're searching and speed up the process when we're creating content.
So to give you a look at, you know, one of our pipelines for augment that is agentic in nature, so it's basically running in a loop until it hits a certain threshold.
What's super cool about this example here is that we're using...
A lot of instructions from the managing editor themselves, right?
They're able to actually provide additional context on the specific assignment that we're writing about.
But we are also going in and we're using, you know, Tavly in this case for the deep research.
But for some clients, right, they request that we use EXA, which is considered to be a bit more superior, but also is different than using like perplexity deep research.
You can see that it's integrating to the knowledge base as well.
And since all of our workflows here are built in code, if I inspect any of these different steps, you'll see that in the background, there's like several things that are happening.
We're generating a research plan.
And you can see that like it's focused on a specific audience.
There's certain questions that we're going in to answer around this specific assignment.
There's success criteria for the research and source requirements.
And then we'll go through and evaluate.
If the quality doesn't meet a certain score, right, we'll run the research again until it's able to meet that standard.
Got it.
So there's a lot that goes into the content creation itself.

And when it comes to like fact checking, we're even able to take the entire article that we draft, separate it into chunks, and then do deep research on every passage to make sure that we can guarantee, right, against the confidence interval that the content is accurate.
And if it's not, it'll suggest a rewrite.
So a lot that goes into the workflows themselves to create content.

But we would be basically working right alongside your team to help you define what are those easy opportunities where you can win and then go out and execute, stage, and publish, provide all of the analytics around how you're performing on a weekly basis against the LLMs.
But also, can we connect our content not just to drive?
Driving traffic, John and James, but driving conversions and being the source of those conversions or the source of those referrals from an AI search.
Tyler, a question for you, and I preface that I ask this of every potential partner I talk to, so it's me playing a little bit of devil's advocate.

I've had, in my marketing tenure, I've had scenarios where I'm working with, for example, you know, a SEO agency with a really preeminent, well-respected founder that is like, knock your socks off, good.

And then what ends up happening is, for whatever reason, we get assigned an account manager that is like three years out of college, and that becomes the, basically the lowest common denominator, right?
So, I appreciate there's a tech component here, but there's always, you know, to some degree, some kind of an account management component.

And I'd love to understand from your perspective, like, how do you ensure that, you know, we're getting kind of the best of both worlds and like where, you know, if we move forward with you that, you know, we're going to have the right kind of partnership to make sure that we're getting the right kind of outcomes to justify the investment.
Yeah, that's a great question.
I would say a couple things.

The first thing would be that the way we've designed the engagements, which I think is different than when you talked to us six months ago, the phase one is intended to be very low risk, because if you're not feeling great about the support that you would get or about the ROI that we'd be able to deliver, you can opt out from the 12-month growth execution.
You would have all of those artifacts available for your own use in addition to the articles that we publish during phase one.
So that's one piece.
The second piece I would say is that we're only kicking off two clients per week intentionally.
And this has been a weird part.
Part my journey at GrowthX is I have to figure out, right, like how to turn people down, basically.
Because at the end of the day, like we want to bring in clients who know that content is the investment that they need.
They're willing to give us the ability to move faster, right?
So if we can't have review cycles that take two or three iterations max, if we can't get in and stage and publish in your CMS, it's hard to create content at the scale, right?
That's needed to be really successful.
And so I think like the two kickoffs a week has ensured that we could scale with quality.
And then the last piece I would say is that right now, like Marcel does not care about revenue.
He cares about churn.
And so if things were taking any sort of dip in the partnership, he would involve himself.
I have no doubt about that, right?
Like you guys would be one of the larger logos that we're working with in addition to Sentinel One.
Auth0 from the Okta team, Ramp, I've seen Marcel involved in every one of those more enterprise relationships.
I hope that helps.
Three different areas to think about a little bit.
Yeah.
And then just remind me, if we're working side by side, as you said, is our content marketing team also accessing the same tools or is that just on your side?

In terms of the platform, our team would be accessing the workflows and creating the content, but your team could review anything that we've drafted and we would take the feedback directly into what the final product looks like.
You're not today licensing the platform to your clients.
You're using it to help scale the clients, but we're not using your platform.
We're interacting with you guys and you're getting the output to us.
Is that the way to...
Exactly.
Exactly.
And I think in like a follow-up, you know, deeper dive with Marcel, it'd be good to ask him some questions around his vision for what, as a client, your involvement would be in the platform.

Because outside of, you know, seeing the analytics and seeing the assignments that we're being working, that we're going to be working on the progress, I'm not entirely sure what the vision is beyond that, other than more of the project management and analytics components.
Okay.
I know we're at time here.
Any last questions for Tyler?

And then I guess the question would be, you're giving, you sort of mentioned your pipeline of, or kind of ability to scale and take on a couple clients a week, what your horizon looks like for the next ones that you're bringing on?
Because we, as I said, we're October 15th, we're going live.
There's probably going to be some shakeout that we're going to need to kind of.
Go through, but, you know, and I want to obviously circle with James and Chanel on this, but supposing we wanted to get started sooner than later, what would that look like?
Yeah, definitely.
So I'm doing my best to, like, make sure partnerships like these are going to be able to have time that's more flexible, right, versus, like, every seed stage startup that we talk to.
And so I would imagine that we could get you guys in before Thanksgiving, assuming that you give direction on the path forward in the next week or so.
And if we need to set up a meeting with Marcel next week, then I think that's, you know, totally understandable and something that he'd be willing to jump on.
Okay, sounds good.
And I have one last question for you, Tyler, from my end at least.
If you think about what an ideal client looks like for you, that'd be great.
And then the alternative is also useful.
So it's like, well,
Where would you point to an example where somebody just wasn't a good fit and you had to part ways because of whatever reason?
I'd love to understand that side of the coin.
Yeah, definitely.
I would say a good fit is a company that knows who their ICP is.
They understand or have a point of view on the topics that they want to create content around and be the authority for.
They are able to define who those specific people are that we need to meet with as part of the review process and what the path to approval looks like.
They understand that there's value in investing in content and that scale helps in this new world.
And I would say that those are kind of like the big pieces, right?
They're willing to collaborate.
On the other side, I would say company without domain authority that thinks we're a silver bullet to...
Okay.
you.
Their growth challenge, company that is going to scrutinize like every single word in tremendous detail and is a real bottleneck to being able to scale.
Those are probably the biggest pieces.
Okay.
Makes sense.
Thank you.
